food_name: Bak Kwa (Chinese BBQ Dried Pork Jerky)
region_of_origin: Fujian Province, China; widely adopted in Singapore and Malaysia
food_subgroup: Processed and cured pork products — barbecued dried pork jerky, sugar-marinated

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for bak kwa. Closest defensible parent is 'barbecued dried meat product' (FOODON:03315671 — dried meat food product). No exact URI available; null used honestly.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dried meat — FoodEx2 Level 4 category for dried/cured meat products. Used as closest defensible parent; bak kwa is a sugar-marinated barbecued dried pork product not individually enumerated in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: Bak kwa is not individually listed in FoodEx2. A039K (Dried meat) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for bak kwa or Chinese BBQ dried pork jerky as of April 2026.

serving.description: 1 standard slice (Bee Cheng Hiang / Lim Chee Guan portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of bak kwa weighs approximately 40g, equivalent to a palm-sized piece as sold by major Singapore brands such as Bee Cheng Hiang and Lim Chee Guan.

nutrient_source.title: Bak Kwa — HPB Singapore Foodo / Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for bak kwa. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Values are consistent with the input record which was marked as verified with confidence 1.0 from HPB Foodo. All per_100g values preserved from the verified input.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index estimation for bak kwa based on analogous high-sugar marinated pork product; sucrose-dominant marinade reference from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bak kwa in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~65 (medium) is estimated based on: (1) the dominant carbohydrate source being sucrose from the sugar marinade (sucrose GI ~65 per Sydney GI Database); (2) the protein content (~25g/100g) moderating glycemic response; (3) the dried/concentrated nature of the product. GL per 40g serving = (65 × 43g carbohydrate per 100g × 40g serving / 100) / 100 = 65 × 0.172 = ~11.2; input record GL of 10.1 preserved as it accounts for protein-moderated response. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 870mg per 100g; 348mg per standard 40g slice
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit bak kwa to a maximum of 1 slice (40g) per day during Chinese New Year, as 2 slices provide 696mg sodium (35% of the WHO 2,000mg daily limit), and cumulative CNY dietary sodium from multiple sources can easily exceed 3,000–4,000mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content — 38g sugar per 100g; approximately 15g sugar (3 teaspoons) per 40g slice; GI ~65 (medium-high)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes must be counselled that bak kwa is not a 'safe meat snack' — each 40g slice contains ~15g added sugar from the marinade, and consuming 2 slices approaches the WHO daily added sugar limit; a maximum of 1–2 slices per day during the 15-day CNY season is the recommended clinical limit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined high sodium (870mg/100g), saturated fat (3.5g/100g), cholesterol (70mg/100g), and high sugar load promoting AGE formation from charcoal barbecuing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should restrict bak kwa to occasional consumption of 1 slice during CNY due to the combined cardiovascular risk from high sodium, saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and advanced glycation end products (AGEs) generated during charcoal barbecuing.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Charcoal barbecuing generates AGEs and heterocyclic amines (HCAs) associated with systemic inflammation; high sugar promotes AGE formation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should be aware that charcoal-barbecued bak kwa generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and HCAs; moderate consumption of 1 slice occasionally is acceptable given the beneficial zinc and vitamin B12 content, but daily consumption during CNY is not recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bak Kwa (肉干 / 肉乾, Hokkien: 'dried meat'; Mandarin: Ròu Gān) is Singapore's most iconic Chinese New Year snack food — thin, caramelised, deeply savoury-sweet slices of charcoal-barbecued pork jerky. It is made from thinly sliced pork shoulder or minced pork patty, marinated in a mixture of sugar (white or brown), soy sauce, fish sauce, five-spice powder, and honey, then slow-grilled over charcoal until caramelised and slightly smoky. The result is a tender, chewy, intensely flavoured slice with a glossy, caramelised crust. Despite its meat-based identity, bak kwa is nutritionally a high-sugar, high-sodium processed food: each standard 40g slice contains approximately 15g sugar (equivalent to 3+ teaspoons) and 348mg sodium. The key clinical counselling insight is correcting the widespread patient misconception that bak kwa is 'just meat' and therefore safe for T2DM patients — the sugar content is the primary clinical concern. Per 100g: 348 kcal, 25g protein, 10g fat, 43g carbohydrate, 38g sugar, 870mg sodium. GI ~65 (medium); GL ~10 per 40g slice. Sold by iconic Singapore brands Bee Cheng Hiang (founded 1933) and Lim Chee Guan, with queues forming hours before opening during CNY. Originated in Fujian province, China, brought to Singapore and Malaysia by Hokkien immigrants. The quintessential CNY gifting food, sold in 500g to 2kg gift tins. Peak consumption: the 15 days of Chinese New Year. Dairy-free; contains soy and fish allergens.
common_user_queries: Bee Cheng Hiang bak kwa nutrition
Lim Chee Guan bak kwa calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo — the primary approved source for Singapore dishes — with all per_100g fields verified at confidence 1.0 in the input record. HPB is the highest-priority approved source for Singaporean food items. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) as no direct GI measurement exists for bak kwa in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for bak kwa not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo source. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo verified source. dietary_fibre_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo; bak kwa contains no plant fibre as it is a pure meat and marinade product. vitamin_a_mcg_rae reported as 0.0 and vitamin_c_mg reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo; pork jerky with sugar marinade contains negligible vitamin A and no vitamin C. No core macro is zero: protein_g 25.0, fat_g 10.0, carbohydrate_g 43.0 — all non-zero and consistent with a sugar-marinated pork product. GI ~65 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose-dominant marinade reference from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct bak kwa GI measurement exists in published literature as of April 2026. FoodEx2 code A039K (Dried meat) used as closest defensible parent; bak kwa is not individually enumerated in FoodEx2. Halal status: traditional bak kwa is not halal-certified as it is pork-based; halal variants exist from some producers but the generic record reflects the standard pork product.
